original_reasoning: Arabic centers on a halter or tying-cord; Greek is a flax-worker, and flax is a prime cord-making material, yielding a solid tool-material chain.
Strengthening and drawing gently both fit measured order and regular motion quite well.
Arabic means restraining suckling by placing a stick in the mouth, and Greek means had or held; both meet in the concept of holding back or restraining.
Arabic رمص includes the sense of making right or repairing, and Greek ἁρμός is a joint; both meet on fastening and joining.
حلكمة is blackness, and melancholy is classically tied to black bile; the mafhum chain from blackness to a dark humor is clear.
Small dates align well with pastries or seeds as small edible items; the semantic class is close.
Arabic taking or handling supports Greek receptions through a clear action to result chain.
حشي carries the sense of being filled or padded, which reaches the notion of bedding.
